story:
It's not that I hate being his best friend. It's that I'm always the backup—the seat-filler, the one behind the camera. The one who says yes because maybe second place is better than no place at all.

Another text: "Sorry, man. Jake said he can hang tonight. Rain check?" The typing dots blink, a punchline I've heard before. I laugh, but it's hollow. I used to joke about being the sidekick. Now it just feels like who I am—and who I'll stay—unless something changes.

At school I'm invisible unless I'm next to Andrew. Then I hear it—Andrew laughing with Jake. "Why do you let that loser tail you?" Jake says. A pause, two seconds that stretch forever. "Stop that," Andrew says, his voice light, wobbly. "Oliver's…cool?" With a question mark. I bolt to the nearest bathroom, lock myself in a stall, press my fists to my eyes until color explodes.

I'm tired of being the shadow—the one who gives rides, loans money, lets him copy homework. Tired of giving and never coming first. A thought worms in: What if Andrew didn't have a choice? What if I stripped away the noise—Jake, the team, the girls, even his parents—so he would finally see who's always been there?

It sounds terrible. It also sounds simple. He trusts me. He gets in my car without thinking, hands me his phone when it dies, counts on me when he's been drinking. My house is always empty; my parents are gone more than they're home. Quiet rooms, a basement nobody uses. I tell myself it's not crazy—it's proof. Proof I matter. But that's a lie. It's obsession pretending to be love.

Friday night, I invite him over. "Just chill." He shows up late, like always, breezy and loud, scrolling his phone, laughing at someone else's joke in my living room. I pour drinks to look normal. My ears ring. My mind screams: Not the dream. Alone. Always alone. Except when you're here.

I move before I can think. A blow. A shatter. Andrew gasps, crumples. The world goes quiet. Relief hits first—and that's the worst part. I tell myself it worked. He's dazed but breathing. I haul him downstairs and restrain him. No tutorials. No glory. Just a line crossed, and crossed again.

In the basement, I secure him tightly to a chair. I circle like a hunter pretending to be a friend. "You're mine now," I whisper. The house above us holds its breath. A warmth spreads through the hollows I've carried for years, but it's not love. It's control. It's the kind of warmth that burns.

Andrew groans awake—confused, then terrified. "Oliver, what is this? Let me go!" I grab his chin, make him look at me. "You've always been everyone's first pick," I spit. "And I'm supposed to wait my turn?" He tries to talk. I cover his mouth. My voice shakes with anger I've fed for years. "Now there's no Jake, no team, no one else to choose over me. You're going to learn what it's like when I'm the only one who decides whether you matter."

He doesn't deserve this. No one does. The story I tell myself—that this is justice, that he'll finally see me—is a lie I use to excuse a crime. Still, I lean closer, my smile twisted. "Don't worry, Andrew. I'll still be your friend."
